# Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer leaves Trump's cabinet after abuse of power allegations

- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

15h ago15 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 5:20am

![Lori Chaves-DeRemer wearing a black blazer.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9ae60a9e7e3667371903cbeaaea6956d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1756&cropW=3122&xPos=0&yPos=162&width=862&height=485)

Lori Chavez-DeRemer at the Oval Office in 2025.(AP: Alex Brandon/ File)

## In short:

Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer is leaving US President Donald Trump's cabinet, after multiple allegations of abusing her position's power, including having an affair with a subordinate and drinking alcohol on the job.

The Labor secretary's departure follows Mr Trump firing his embattled homeland security secretary, Kristi Noem, in March and ousting attorney-general Pam Bondi earlier this month.

## What's next?

Deputy Labor Secretary Keith Sonderling will become the acting Labor secretary in Ms Chavez DeRemer's place.

US Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer is leaving Donald Trump's cabinet after multiple allegations of abusing her position's power, including having an affair with a subordinate and drinking alcohol on the job.

Ms Chavez-DeRemer is the third Trump cabinet member to leave her post this year: the US president fired his embattled homeland security secretary, Kristi Noem, in March and ousted attorney-general Pam Bondi earlier this month.

In a statement posted on social media, Ms Chavez-DeRemer praised Mr Trump and wrote: "I am proud that we made significant progress in advancing President Trump's mission to bridge the gap between business and Labor and always put the American worker first."

Unlike other recent cabinet departures, Ms Chavez-DeRemer's exit was announced by a White House aide on Monday, local time, and not by the president on his social media account.

"Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer will be leaving the Administration to take a position in the private sector," White House communications director Steven Cheung said on the social media site X.

![Donald Trump shows a document to Lori Chavez-DeRemer and other officials.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/441b711298b4e3b3e48d8ac18cc92c17?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2473&cropW=3709&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Lori Chavez-DeRemer praised Donald Trump in her resignation.(Reuters: Jonathan Ernst)

"She has done a phenomenal job in her role by protecting American workers, enacting fair Labor practices, and helping Americans gain additional skills to improve their lives."

He said Keith Sonderling, the current deputy Labor secretary, would become acting Labor secretary in her place.

The news outlet NOTUS was the first to report Ms Chavez-DeRemer's resignation.

## Labor chief, family members faced multiple allegations

Ms Chavez-DeRamer's departure follows reports that began surfacing in January that she was under a series of investigations.

A New York Times report last Wednesday revealed that the Labor Department's inspector-general was reviewing material showing Ms Chavez-DeRemer and her top aides and family members routinely sent personal messages and requests to young staff members.

Ms Chavez-DeRemer's husband and father exchanged text messages with young female staff members, according to the newspaper.

Some of the staffers were instructed by the secretary and her former deputy chief of staff to "pay attention" to her family, people familiar with the investigation told the Times.

Those messages were uncovered as part of a broader investigation of Ms Chavez-DeRemer's leadership that began after the New York Post reported in January that a complaint filed with the Labor Department's inspector-general accused Ms Chavez-DeRemer of a relationship with the subordinate.

She also faced allegations that she drank alcohol on the job and that she tasked aides to plan official trips for primarily personal reasons.

Late on Monday, on her personal X account, Ms Chavez-DeRemer posted: "The allegations against me, my family, and my team have been peddled by high-ranked deep state actors who have been coordinating with the one-sided news media and continue to undermine \[US\] President Trump's mission."

Both the White House and the Labor Department initially said the reports of wrongdoing were baseless.

But the official denials got less full-throated as more allegations emerged, and when Ms Chavez-DeRemer might be out of a job became something of an open question in Washington.

At least four Labor Department officials were forced from their jobs as the investigation progressed, including Ms Chavez-DeRemer's former chief of staff and deputy chief of staff, as well as a member of her security detail, with whom she was accused of having an affair, The New York Times reported.

"I think the secretary demonstrated a lot of wisdom in resigning," Senator John Kennedy said on Monday after her departure was made public.

Ms Chavez-DeRemer, who is a former House GOP member, had represented a swing district in Oregon and was confirmed to Trump's cabinet on a 67-32 vote in March 2025.

In her single term in Congress, Ms Chavez-DeRemer backed legislation that would make it easier to unionise on a federal level, as well as a separate bill aimed at protecting social security benefits for public-sector employees.

## Key figure in Trump's deregulatory push

Aside from reports of wrongdoing in recent months, Ms Chavez-DeRemer had been one of Mr Trump's more low-profile cabinet picks, but took key steps to advance the administration's deregulatory agenda during her tenure.

For instance, the Labor Department last year moved to rewrite or repeal more than 60 workplace regulations it saw as obsolete. The roll backs included minimum wage requirements for home health care workers and people with disabilities, and rules governing exposure to harmful substances and safety procedures at mines.

The effort drew condemnation from union leaders and workplace safety experts.

The proposed changes also included eliminating a requirement that employers provide adequate lighting for construction sites and seat belts for agriculture workers in most employer-provided transportation.

During Ms Chavez-DeRemer's tenure, the Trump administration cancelled millions of dollars in international grants that a Labor Department division administered to combat child labour and slave labour around the world, ending their work that had helped reduce the number of child labourers worldwide by 78 million over the past two decades.

In her statement on Monday, Ms Chavez-DeRemer said: "While my time serving in the administration comes to a conclusion, it doesn't mean I will stop fighting for American workers."

The Labor Department has a broad mandate as it relates to the US workforce, including reporting the US unemployment rate, regulating workplace health and safety standards, investigating minimum wage, child labour and overtime pay disputes, and applying laws on union organising and unlawful terminations.

**AP**

news.snippet: For months seafarers have been stranded in the Strait of Hormuz, tanker captain Raman Kapoor is speaking out about the fears and uncertainty of his crew.

# Captain stranded in the Strait of Hormuz tells of being trapped amid conflict

- [![Norman Hermant is the ABC’s Moscow correspondent.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/180b5a8e58e769129e9352df9dbc2bca?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=600&cropW=600&xPos=0&yPos=86&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
N\\
\\
By Norman Hermant](https://www.abc.net.au/news/norman-hermant/166882)
- [7.30](https://www.abc.net.au/news/programs/730/)
- [Topic:Unrest, Conflict and War](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/unrest-conflict-and-war)

10h ago10 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 5:09am

![A man wearing a ship's captain uniform.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4f1499de34fc83300e30d997f3ed6758?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=389&cropW=692&xPos=92&yPos=12&width=862&height=485)

Raman Kapoor is the captain of an oil tanker which has been stuck in the Persian Gulf since the start of the war. (Instagram)

For Captain Raman Kapoor, the days aboard his oil tanker now have a dangerous familiarity, anchored on the edge of war.

"My ship is anchored in the north part of the Persian Gulf, and my ship is a crude oil tanker," Captain Kapoor told 7.30.

"We are here ever since the war started on the 28th \[of\] February."

Captain Kapoor won't reveal his precise location due to security fears, but the war is close to him and his crew.

They have witnessed strikes occur.

![A large tanker sits on the ocean against a mountainous landscape.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2dc008332990ff95ea6467f3b5f7d6c1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=4220&xPos=390&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Thousands of seafarers are stranded near the Strait of Hormuz. (File photo via Reuters)

"We have noticed various missiles in the past and various explosion sounds and attacks. Many attacks on merchant ships," he said.

> "One of them was very close to our ship. So of course everyone is scared."

His tanker has a crew of 24, and the captain said one of the biggest challenges was maintaining morale — especially amid reports that the strait's status was changing by the day as the US and Iran continued their dispute.

"A couple of days back, Iran opened the Strait of Hormuz, and then the situation was pretty good, and news brought major relief to all my crew, and there was less tension and more hope," he said.

![Several large tankers and ships in an open ocean.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d1ebc4e9fa5527dc35afc25b20baec5f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3267&cropW=4901&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Oil tankers and cargo ships sit off the coast of the United Arab Emirates. (AP: Altaf Qadri/File)

"But within 24 hours, we heard that again the Strait of Hormuz is closed by Iran, and in fact, they also attacked two ships."

The situation in the strait remains unclear, with the US continuing to blockade Iranian ports.

Tehran has not committed to a new round of peace talks and traffic in the strait remains at a fraction of pre-war levels.

"The relief that we got after the reopening of the strait was turned again into uncertainty, and yes, my crew is again worried," Captain Kapoor said.

## Worsening conditions

![The sun rises behind a ship sitting on sea with puffy clouds above](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d5ef0eee97533858c6fcee9339c8888b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Seafarers stuck in the Persian Gulf have no idea how much longer they'll have to wait there. (AP: Asghar Besharati)

All of the crew on Captain Kapoor's ship are Indian. They are just some of the more than 20,000 Indians working on ships in the Persian Gulf, according to Manoj Yadav, the General Secretary of the Forward Seaman's Union of India.

They not only work as crew on oil tankers and cargo ships, but also on support ships that supply oil and gas platforms in the Persian Gulf.

"Thousands of seafarers, mainly Indian seafarers … are working on \[these\] small craft, barges, tugboats, offshore vessels, which are all operating within the oil field in the middle conflict zone," Mr Yadav told 7.30.

"The seafarers were under big fear because the targeted area is very close to their ships.

> "You can say Iranian navy and the coast guard vessels are very close, even within a 500-metre or one-kilometre range. These vessels got targeted."

As the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has continued, Mr Yadav says the conditions for crews stuck on ships offshore or in port aree worsening.

![A satellite photo of a lot of large ships floating in the sea.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ff3c5ef9916068fc9ad15a6e6aa014b7?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2000&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Ships floating off the coast of Dubai. (Supplied: Sentinel Hub)

"They were feeling just like a jail nearly 30 days or 40 days on board without \[being allowed\] to go outside, without \[being allowed\] to communicate.

"Many \[ports have\] restricted their communication also not to share photos, not to share videos.

"Many other conditions were also imposed …  many seafarers face so many challenges, shortage of food is one of them."

## Iran's missile-laden coastline

One of the other challenges of navigating the strait right now is the risk for owners of ships when it comes to the safety of their vessels.

Jakob Larsen, head of maritime safety and security at BIMCO, the world's largest association of shipowners based in Copenhagen, believes many will be reluctant to send their ships through until a peace deal is negotiated.

![Dozens of ships sit still in the Strait of Hormuz, with a steep slope in the background. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/3fc933b182ac118fe6d9c5a786f570f0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2592&cropW=3888&xPos=360&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Seafarers are heading into their third month of being stuck in the Gulf. (Reuters)

"I think for shipping to resume, we are going to need to see that the warring parties have a … reasonable chance that they actually agree on a peace deal, and then traffic can start to move," Mr Larsen told 7.30.

"In the short-term, I don't see that happening. The gap between the two is just too big."

Mr Larsen believes even the US Navy will struggle to force the strait open.

"Iran controls the whole northern coastline of the Strait of Hormuz," he said.

"It is composed of mountainous terrain, so it's very difficult to get a sufficient level of military control in that area for the US.

"Iran has dispersed their anti-ship missile capability along the whole coastline, which is more than 500 kilometres.

"So, Iran still retains the capability to project sea power into the Strait of Hormuz … and (US Navy) escorts are not going to change that."

Despite that, Mr Larsen believes there will always be a small minority of owners willing to risk sending their ships through the strait.

"Every ship owner has different risk acceptance criteria," he said.

"We never go into advising on specific transits and so on, but what we can do is advise about the threat, and currently the threat is too high."

Until that threat level changes, oil tankers like the one commanded by Captain Kapoor will stay at anchor, with his crew entering their third month waiting for safe passage through the Strait.

"There is a very volatile situation at the moment," he said.

"Of course, there is always an end to any war. But when it will happen nobody knows.

> "We are waiting, we are being optimistic — and waiting for the right moment."

**Watch** [**7.30**](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/7-30) **, Mondays to Thursdays 7:30pm on** [**ABC iview**](https://iview.abc.net.au/show/7-30) **and ABC TV**

# Israel's occupation of south Lebanon spells 'bleak' future despite ceasefire, experts say

By [Basel Hindeleh](https://www.abc.net.au/news/basel-hindeleh/102012326), and [Cherine Yazbeck](https://www.abc.net.au/news/cherine-yazbeck/104216464) in Lebanon

- [Topic:War](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/war)

13h ago13 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 12:56am

![Smoke rising from buildings on a hillside](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6be3b90695d5648eebfb83fcb1ad101c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2754&cropW=4896&xPos=0&yPos=332&width=862&height=485)

Smoke rises following an explosion in a Lebanese town. (Reuters: Florion Goga)

Israel and Lebanon are set to meet for further negotiations later this week as the 10-day ceasefire with Hezbollah enters its second half.

But while talks of what comes next take place, experts say Israel's continued presence in south Lebanon is creating a "bleak" future.

The Israeli military is now occupying a belt of territory at the border between the two countries — telling residents to stay out, while continuing to destroy infrastructure inside many villages.

And come next week, regardless of the outcome of the negotiations, experts believe Lebanon will be facing a "new reality" in the south that it did not want.

## Israel's occupation and new 'Yellow Line'

On Sunday the Israel Defense Forces (IDF) released a map highlighting a large area hugging the border in southern Lebanon which it said would remain under its control for the duration of the ceasefire.

The [map shows an area stretching east to west along the entire border](https://x.com/AvichayAdraee/status/2046092202414149706), running between five and 10 kilometres deep into Lebanese territory and covering about 560 square kilometres — or about 5.5 per cent of Lebanon's land.

Israeli military control in Lebanon

A map of southern Lebanon showing an area inside its borders controlled by the Israeli military.

Showing a low-resolution version of the map. Make sure your browser supports WebGL to see the full version.

![Israeli military control in Lebanon ](https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/Pqe5X/plain.png)

Approximation based on imprecise IDF image.

ABC News: Basel HindelehSource:IDF

The IDF has called the boundary its "Forward Defense Line" and the area a "buffer zone" where it said troops would work to "dismantle Hezbollah terror infrastructure sites and to prevent direct threats to communities in northern Israel".

But it has also called it Lebanon's "Yellow Line", a term it used to describe the ceasefire border in Gaza.

Michael Young, a senior editor at the Malcolm H Kerr Carnegie Middle East Center in Beirut, said the boundary represented the current front line with Hezbollah, but likely not its final form.

"For the moment this is a temporary line. I think the Israelis will try gradually to establish a line that is more defensible, that isn't a line that was imposed on them by a ceasefire," he said.

Beyond the occupied area, Mr Young argued Israel may seek to exert a level of control in other parts of the south where it could fire freely and "act when they want".

> "I don't think the Israelis will ever give up on their ability to strike targets inside Lebanon,"

he said.

Late last week Hezbollah indicated it was willing to abide by the ceasefire so long as it included a "comprehensive halt to attacks" across Lebanon and "no freedom of movement for Israeli forces".

But after the IDF released the map of its new "security zone" Hezbollah said it would fight to "bring down" the line.

"The attempt by the Israeli army to establish a buffer zone, under the title of a defensive line, a yellow line, a green line, and a red line … all these lines will be broken, and we will not accept any of them," Hezbollah politician Hassan Fadlallah told AFP overnight, vowing that "no-one in Lebanon or abroad will be able to disarm the resistance".

The IDF said it had already attacked "terrorists in several areas" for approaching Israeli forces from north of the Yellow Line, adding that its actions were in self-defence and therefore did not violate the new ceasefire.

Another statement from the IDF said, "At this stage, Lebanese civilians are not yet permitted to return to southern Lebanon."

Israel's stated "buffer zone" also stretches out to sea and carves up a portion of Lebanon's exclusive economic zone which contains potential gas fields.

While analysts say it is a worrying aspect of the occupation, it does not affect the maritime border agreed between Lebanon and Israel in 2022.

"From a legal point of view, this map doesn't change anything about the fact that there is a maritime border agreement," Lebanese Energy Minister Joe Saddi told Reuters.

"Very simply, the agreement is in effect and nothing is changing."

## Destruction in southern Lebanon

South of the Yellow Line the IDF has already begun destruction in some of the dozens of villages that were told to evacuate earlier in the war.

"There is a ceasefire now, but they're still blowing up houses," Adnan Alyan, the former mayor of the Shia Muslim-majority town of Khiam told the ABC.

Mr Alyan currently resides in Lebanon's capital, Beirut, but his family home in Khiam was destroyed at the beginning of the war.

"They go in, they blow up the entire neighbourhood, 30 or 40 houses, they bomb them all together," he said.

"They're bombing every day, everywhere in the south. They created a belt and they're bombing them all."

Ali Hassan Khalil, a top aide to Lebanon's Parliament Speaker Nabih Berri, said Israel has carried out varying degrees of destruction in 39 villages during the ceasefire, including destroying civilian homes.

He said this amounted to "a clear war crime".

The IDF said its operations targeted Hezbollah infrastructure, but sources [told Haaretz](https://www.haaretz.com/israel-news/israel-security/2026-04-19/ty-article/.premium/like-gaza-idf-razes-south-lebanon-villages-during-cease-fire-sources-say/0000019d-a43d-d31c-a1df-ae3f8a660000) that civilian infrastructure — including homes, public buildings and schools — were being flattened, and [a BBC analysis of satellite imagery](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cdxkk1vnp57o) showed complete villages destroyed.

Earlier this week Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz said the destruction of houses along the border would continue despite the ceasefire, saying without providing evidence that they had "effectively become terrorist outposts".

"Israel is implementing a strategy in Lebanon reminiscent of its tactics in Gaza," Sibylle Rizk, a Lebanese analyst and journalist, told the ABC.

"The systematic destruction of occupied localities signals an intent for a prolonged occupation aimed at minimising Israeli casualties from Hezbollah's guerilla operations."

She said the IDF was "opting for a 'scorched earth' approach" rather than risking "urban combat on a terrain mastered by" Hezbollah.

"For Lebanon, the cost of this new occupation, following the 1978–2000 period \[during which Israel occupied parts of southern Lebanon\], is an unbearable burden for the southern population," she said.

The IDF has claimed Hezbollah embeds military infrastructure and assets, including weapons, inside civilian areas and homes.

It also [told BBC News](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cdxkk1vnp57o) it did not destroy property unless militarily necessary.

## Christian villages also face 'crisis'

Israel's destruction operations have so far largely focused on Shia Muslim towns where there is support for Hezbollah.

Southern Lebanon is also home to several Christian villages which have mostly been left intact and where some residents have remained during the war.

But their future is also in doubt.

Over the weekend a photo emerged showing an Israeli soldier taking the blunt side of an axe to a fallen sculpture of Jesus on the cross in the Christian town of Debel.

![A soldier in uniform swings the blunt side of an axe at a fallen sculpture of Jesus on a corss](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/163ac8805bc9fcbf232133dd785c1e51?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1066&cropW=1066&xPos=0&yPos=134&width=862&height=862)

An Israeli soldier damages the head of a statue of Jesus, in Debel. (Social Media via Reuters)

The soldier's actions were strongly condemned by both Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and the IDF, which said the soldier's conduct was "wholly inconsistent with the values expected of its troops".

The cross was part of a small shrine in the garden of a family living on the edge of the village, local priest Fadi Falfel told Reuters.

He called it a "desecration of our holy symbols".

"We have every kind of crisis," he said.

"We thought the ceasefire would bring us some relief but we're still surrounded, unable to travel to and from the town. There are some houses on the edge of town that we're barred from accessing."

While some Christian residents fear their villages could be destroyed if they leave, Mr Young says Israel's current strategy is to leave them alone and use their presence for its advantage.

"If people in \[the Christian village of\] Ramesh don't want their village to be destroyed then eventually any kind of activity by Hezbollah could be opposed by the people of the village because it could potentially threaten them," he said.

"The Israelis are pitting the communities against each other. This is very simple."

The [IDF told BBC News](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cdxkk1vnp57o) that any suggestion it was punishing civilian communities or targeting civilians on the basis of religion was "categorically false".

## 'Bleak' future for Lebanon

Representatives of Israel and Lebanon will meet in Washington DC on Thursday to discuss a more permanent peace treaty.

But Lebanon, which found itself dragged into the war in early March when Hezbollah opened fire over the Israeli border in support of Iran, is negotiating from a weaker position.

Analyst Sybille Rizk said that Lebanon was trying to negotiate a Hezbollah disarmament following a full Israeli withdrawal, while the US and Israel hinge a withdrawal on Hezbollah's disarmament.

"To prevail at the negotiating table Lebanon desperately needs allies to act as a counterweight to both Israel's military pressure and Iran's heightened control over Hezbollah's strategic decisions", Ms Rizk said.

"Without a diplomatic breakthrough ensuring a full withdrawal and a massive international reconstruction plan, these villages risk becoming a permanent, depopulated 'no-man's land' effectively severed from the Lebanese state and its economy."

She added that the maritime boundary was also under threat because it was "inextricably linked to the coastal land terminus" and with the occupation.

"Israel is effectively laying claim to a portion of Lebanon's maritime zone, which is potentially rich in hydrocarbons," she said.

"While Lebanon has international law on its side, Israel is asserting the logic of force. This remains a pivotal issue for any future settlement."

Mr Young said Lebanon was being forced to negotiate because it "wants to liberate its territory".

"But at the same time what will be negotiated between the sides will be determined by what the Israelis want to do with their occupation zone," he said.

More than 800,000 Lebanese have been displaced from the south during the current war, according to the UN, and most are not being allowed to return.

At least 2,300 people have been killed, including 117 children, as crews search for the remains of others.

Hezbollah has not disclosed its casualty figures, but at least 400 of its fighters had been killed by the end of March, according to sources.

Israel said two civilians were killed in Israel during Hezbollah's initial attack when the Iran war broke out, while 15 Israeli soldiers have died in Lebanon since March 2.

Mr Young believes that Israel is trying to create a "a new reality" by forcing a pre-determined outcome on the negotiations through its occupation of Lebanon's south.

"I see that the future of these villages is very bleak," he said.

"The Israelis want to create a sentiment that this \[buffer zone\] is a permanent structure and that … if one day they eventually withdraw, that these areas will still remain under Israel's eye.

"Israel wants to seek to impose the new reality, the new arrangements for habitation of this area."

## Hopes that the 'south will be rebuilt'

Israel has yet to say when or if it will leave southern Lebanon, but in a video statement earlier this week Mr Netanyahu said Israeli troops intended to remain in the area during the ceasefire.

"We are staying in Lebanon in a reinforced security zone," he said, adding: "We are not leaving."

Mr Katz also said earlier this week that the IDF was not planning to withdraw from Lebanon any time soon.

"The IDF holds and will continue to hold all the areas it has cleared and captured," he said.

![a view of a hillside village with many houses demolished with heavy machinery operating and an Israeli flag in the centre](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1c3df296e5210b2c1d43fb8b726a1c95?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2694&cropW=4040&xPos=323&yPos=639&width=862&height=575)

An Israeli flag flies on top of a damaged building in Lebanese territory as heavy machinery operates amid rubble. (Reuters: Florion Goga)

The security of civilians in northern Israel, an area which is often targeted by Hezbollah rockets when fighting breaks out, remains Israel's "top priority", the IDF said in a recent statement.

But the occupation, Ms Rizk argues, "constitutes a flagrant violation of international law and Lebanon's territorial integrity, regardless of Israel's security justifications."

"The central challenge for Lebanese authorities is defending a sovereignty they cannot enforce on the ground," she said.

As the question of Israel's withdrawal from Lebanon remains up in the air, former Khiam mayor Adnan Alyan remains hopeful that the "south will be rebuilt".

> "God willing, everyone will return. They will retreat again and the entire south will be rebuilt,"

he said.

"It's all worth the sacrifice for the resistance and Lebanon, but the most important thing is our dignity to stay intact."

# El Salvador holds mass trial of nearly 500 alleged MS-13 gang members

- [Topic:Courts](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/courts)

17h ago17 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:49am

![A large group of men with shaved heads wear masks and matching clothing while sitting on plastic chairs in a prison.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e20ec5ad0c545bfeeb2529e0aad33596?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2315&cropW=4116&xPos=0&yPos=217&width=862&height=485)

Members of Mara Salvatrucha, also known as MS-13 gang, attend a mass trial via video link. (Reuters: Attorney General of El Salvador)

## In short:

Hundreds of alleged members of the Central American gang Mara Salvatrucha (MS-13) have gone on trial collectively in El Salvador.

Prosecutors allege hundreds of suspected MS-13 members committed 47,000 crimes between 2012 and 2022, including 29,000 homicides.

## What's next?

Anonymous judges will hand down a one-size-fits-all punishment to the large group of defendants.

Nearly 490 alleged members of the Mara Salvatrucha (MS-13) gang have been collectively tried in El Salvador.

The Central American country is conducting mass trials of thousands of suspected gang members, many of whom have spent years in prison without charge or visiting rights.

The trials are part of President Nayib Bukele's anti-gang crackdown.

The attorney-general's office said 486 suspected MS-13 members were on trial for 47,000 crimes, including 29,000 homicides, committed between 2012 and 2022.

The court system said the trial included "members of the national leadership, street-level leaders, program coordinators from across the country, and founders" of MS-13.

![Men with shaved heads wear masks and matching outfits as they sit together in a room.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/62830904cd5a0d908c57d63261e8adc1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2827&cropW=4240&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The prisoners in the mass trial will be handed a one-size-fits-all sentence. (Reuters: Attorney General of El Salvador)

## 'Settle a historic debt'

Salvadoran authorities accuse the group of a range of crimes, including the killing of 87 people in a single weekend in March 2022.

In the wake of those killings, Mr Bukele, a close ally of US President Donald Trump, declared a "war" on gangs, which he said controlled 80 per cent of Salvadoran territory.

MS-13 is charged with the crime of rebellion "because they sought to … establish a parallel state," the attorney-general's office said.

"We are going to put them on trial, and we are going to settle a historic debt," prosecutors said.

In 2022, Mr Bukele imposed a state of emergency, which has been used to arrest more than 91,000 suspected gang members, including thousands of people who were later declared innocent.

The campaign, which made Mr Bukele hugely popular, has resulted in a dramatic decrease in crime, turning El Salvador from one of Latin America's most dangerous countries to one of its safest.

Rights groups, however, have denounced gross human rights abuses, including a lack of due process for the detainees, reports of torture and more than 500 deaths in prison.

## Blanket convictions

Anonymous judges are now handing down one-size-fits-all punishments to large groups of defendants after the proceedings via video link from prison.

MS-13 and the rival Barrio 18 gang operate drug trafficking rings and extortion rackets across Central America.

The Trump administration has declared the two groups as terrorist organisations, designations it has used in part to justify deadly military strikes on alleged drug-running boats.

The two gangs were born among Salvadoran youth on the streets of Los Angeles and then spread back to El Salvador, where they terrorised the population for more than three decades.

![A roomful of men with shaved heads and matching clothing sit and look at a screen displaying a video conference call.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d1b096794fa014377c4e15e3b20e18a5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2827&cropW=4240&xPos=0&yPos=3&width=862&height=575)

Several groups of the alleged gang members joined the mass trial online from different locations. (Reuters: Attorney General of El Salvador)

Mr Bukele has accused them of murdering 200,000 people over three decades, including about 80,000 who disappeared without a trace.

State prosecutors said they had "ample evidence to request the maximum sentences" against the defendants, without specifying whether that meant life imprisonment.

At the opening of the trial, the judge stated that armed groups had disturbed "the peace of the Salvadoran population and the security of the state" for decades, and would be tried "with the full force of the law".

New York-based Human Rights Watch and regional NGO Cristosal have criticised the mass trials, warning of the risk of innocent people being made to pay for the crimes of the guilty.

**AFP**

# Formula 1 to make rule changes for Miami Grand Prix after concerns over new engine regulations

- [Topic:Formula 1](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/formula-1)

21h ago21 hours agoMon 20 Apr 2026 at 8:10pm

![The front of Kimi Antonelli's crashed Formula 1 car at the Australian Grand Prix.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/23b44cf9cb844336ad1a7f536f1977df?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=428&width=862&height=485)

The 2026 F1 season has endured a chaotic start. (Getty Images: Marcel van Dorst/EYE4IMAGES/NurPhoto)

## In short:

Formula 1 teams and stakeholders have unanimously agreed to rule tweaks after drivers raised safety concerns over new engine regulations and energy management issues.

The first three races of the 2026 season have been marred by crashes and stall-outs after changes to power units.

## What's next?

Some changes will be implemented for the next Grand Prix in Miami on May 3, while race start adjustments will be trialled.

Formula 1 teams and stakeholders have ​unanimously agreed to engine rule tweaks aimed at improving the racing and driver safety from the next race in Miami on May 3.

[The sport this season started a new era](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-11/formula-1-2026-new-engines-regulations-sprint-races/106325942), with the hybrid power units now split roughly 50-50 between electric and ​combustion power.

Some drivers have complained over ‌the first three races about having to "lift and coast" into high-speed corners so the combustion engine can recharge the battery, and have aired safety concerns regarding the start and speed differentials during the race.

They have also had to ‌contend with "super-clipping", where the power unit automatically diverts energy from the engine to the battery, slowing ⁠down the car even if the driver is flat on the throttle.

It has had such an impact that [four-time world champion Max Verstappen has ​questioned his future in the ‌sport](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-02-13/max-verstappen-critical-of-new-formula-1-cars/106339508).

The governing International Automobile Federation (FIA) said in a statement that an online meeting with the 11 team bosses, power unit CEOs and Formula 1 had agreed on a number of in-season "refinements" that would now be put to a virtual vote.

"The proposals agreed today … will be implemented from Miami, apart from the ​race ‌start changes that will be tested in Miami and adopted following feedback and analysis," it said.

The FIA said energy management parameters would be adjusted for qualifying, a key area ⁠of concern, with a reduction in maximum permitted recharge ‌from eight megajoules to seven to ‌encourage more consistent flat-out driving and peak super-clip power increased from 250kW to 350kW to reduce the time spent recharging.

In ‌the race, measures were introduced to reduce excessive closing speeds and limit sudden performance differentials with the maximum power available through boost capped at an additional 150kW.

A new ‌system has ​been developed and will be ⁠trialled to mitigate the risks of a slow-starting car being struck by a faster one close behind, as well as measures to improve safety and visibility in the wet.

"These are sensible changes and the teams, FIA and Formula 1 have done good work over the past ⁠few weeks to agree them," Williams boss James Vowles said on social media.

"F1 has seen some great racing so far this year, but ‌it is right ‌that we always look at ways to keep improving."

Mercedes boss Toto Wolff had ​earlier told reporters changes needed to be made "with a scalpel and not with a baseball bat" and cautioned against bad-mouthing the sport in public.

# Who is John Ternus, the hardware engineer appointed as new CEO of Apple?

By [Georgie Hewson](https://www.abc.net.au/news/georgie-hewson/13465438) with wires

- [Topic:Consumer Electronics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/consumer-electronics)

22h ago22 hours agoMon 20 Apr 2026 at 9:16pm

![John Ternus presents at a apple hardware unveiling on a stage](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/649364cd02081df690f6cd463b28ca4d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2352&cropW=4181&xPos=0&yPos=712&width=862&height=485)

John Ternus is known for unveiling new hardware at many Apple events. (Reuters: File)

Apple has announced CEO Tim Cook will step down in September, handing the chief executive job to company veteran John Ternus.

Mr Ternus, senior vice-president of hardware engineering, will become Apple's next chief executive officer from September 1.

The transition, which was approved unanimously by Apple's board of directors, comes after what the company said was a thoughtful, long-term succession-planning process.

So who is Mr Ternus and why will his appointment shape the future of one of the world's largest technology companies?

## Who is John Ternus?

![John Ternus speaks about the new Mac Pro during Apple's annual Worldwide Developers Conference in San Jose, California in 2019. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/717dad3d91cb018cc9db419501674b95?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1644&cropW=2465&xPos=234&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

John Ternus speaks at a conference in California in 2019. (Reuters: Mason Trinca)

Mr Ternus joined Apple's product design team in 2001 and became a vice-president of hardware engineering in 2013.

He joined the executive team in 2021 as senior vice-president of hardware engineering.

During his time at Apple, he oversaw the development of hardware on a variety of Apple products including the introduction of the iPad and AirPods

Mr Ternus's work on Mac has helped the "category become more powerful and more popular globally than at any time in its 40-year history", a press release from Apple read.

"Under his leadership, his team also drove advancements in AirPods to make them the world's best in-ear headphones, with unprecedented active noise cancellation, as well as the capability to become an all-in-one hearing health system that can serve as over-the-counter hearing aids."

Apple also credits him with innovation that has reduced the carbon footprint of the company's profits and improved durability.

Prior to Apple, Mr Ternus worked as a mechanical engineer at Virtual Research Systems, an early innovator in the virtual reality space.

He holds a bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from the University of Pennsylvania.

"There's been speculation about who would replace Tim Cook after 15 years and there were two paths they could have gone down," New York Times technology correspondent Mike Isaac told News Channel.

"There's the Steve Jobs version, the more dreamer type that folks have wondered if there's going to be a next visionary, or the more Tim Cook version which is, you know, \[a\] steady, sober-minded person who knows how to manage a huge business.

"And I think John Ternus, the successor, is in the latter camp for sure."

## Transition comes at vital time

![John Ternus in brown hair and dark clothes on the left and Tim Cook wtih grey hair and dark clothes on the right ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/bfb1f02d6c815cfb6e1d2e2a9ab77805?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

John Ternus will replace Tim Cook after a long process of succession planning. (Supplied: Apple)

The transition to a new CEO comes at a pivotal time for Apple.

Artificial intelligence has unleashed the most upheaval within the industry since Mr Jobs unveiled the first iPhone in 2007.

Apple has gotten off to a rough start in AI after stumbling in its efforts to deliver new features built on the technology, as promised nearly two years ago.

Earlier this year, Apple finally turned to Google — an early leader in the AI race — for help making the iPhone's virtual assistant, Siri, into a more conversational and versatile helper.

"Cook created a major legacy at Apple but it was ultimately time to pass the torch to Ternus with the AI strategy now the focus," Wedbush Securities analyst Dan Ives said.

Mr Ternus will also have to fend off Nvidia, which has announced its own personal computer and is working on chips that can power laptops, as well as rivals such as Meta Platforms, whose augmented-reality glasses have become a surprise hit with just a fraction of the capabilities — and price tag — of Apple's Vision Pro headset.

"The promotion of Mr Ternus indicates the company will focus on new hardware devices such as folding phones, glasses, VR devices and AI pins," Gil Luria, managing director of DA Davidson & Co, told Reuters.

## Vital growth under Cook

Mr Ternus's predecessor, Mr Cook, will become the company's executive chairman, Apple said in a statement.

Apple stock has soared twentyfold since Mr Cook took over as CEO from Mr Jobs in 2011.

While Mr Cook made his early reputation at Apple by building out its sprawling supply chain in China, over the years he became a celebrity CEO in his own right.

He was the first Fortune 500 CEO to come out as gay in 2014 and took public stances on issues such as workplace diversity and corporate sustainability.

Mr Cook, who presented a custom golden plaque to US President Donald Trump, will also continue to engage with policymakers, the company said.

In naming Mr Ternus chief executive, Apple is shifting from a supply-chain guru who helped turn Apple into a global brand that churns out hundreds of millions of units per year, to a leader who has long focused on design and products.

"He's built a business that has essentially quadrupled in annual profits since he started, it's worth 10 times as much as it was before his tenure and, you know, he has to pick a moment where he wants to go out and try to go out on top and I think this next era of Apple is really going to be defined by, you know, the AI race," New York Times technology correspondent Mike Isaace told ABC News Channel.

"Apple is on its back foot a little bit and needs to catch up. So perhaps it is a good time for him to let his successor do that."

**ABC/Wires**

# Wollongong researchers working to create shark-deterrent surfboard fins

By [Brooke Chandler](https://www.abc.net.au/news/brooke-chandler/102272044) and [Melinda James](https://www.abc.net.au/listen/programs/backgroundbriefing/melinda-james/5765216)

- [ABC Illawarra](https://www.abc.net.au/illawarra/)
- [Topic:Shark Attacks](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/shark-attacks)

11h ago11 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 3:07am

![Man sanding surfboard in lab, wearing facemask and protective glasses](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/0707d89daa4942256ece6f025724e03a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=260&width=862&height=485)

Marc in het Panhuis is leading the team making shark-deterrent surfboard fins. (ABC Illawarra: Justin Huntsdale)

## In short:

Researchers from the University of Wollongong are working to fit shark-deterrent technology into surfboard fins following recent shark attacks.

Shark attack survivor Brett Connellan has welcomed the project, saying it could help sharks and surfers co-exist.

## What's next?

Researchers are hoping to deliver prototypes by the end of this year before they are commercially produced.

Surfboard fins fitted with shark-deterrent technology are being developed in NSW to help reduce deadly shark encounters.

A $2.5 million research project by the University of Wollongong (UOW), in partnership with the UNSW and Gowing Bros, is working to design and manufacture the product in the wake of recent shark attacks.

Four non-fatal shark bites were recorded in January along the NSW coastline within 48 hours, [sparking the closure of dozens of beaches.](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-01-21/what-australian-shark-attack-data-reveals/106248228)

![Hand holding blue surfboard fins with wires at the bottom](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/733ff1695dbe9521399e0c9fcb21efb5?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=4435&cropW=3326&xPos=7&yPos=0&width=862&height=1149)

The smart surfboard fins will be embedded with electromagnetic systems, miniaturised sensors and illumination features. (ABC Illawarra: Justin Huntsdale)

"This is an industry response to shark attacks in Australia over the past 12 months," UOW Surf Flex Lab lead Marc in het Panhuis said.

The project, co-funded by the Australian Composites Manufacturing Cooperative Research Centre, aims to incorporate established technologies designed to inhibit a shark's ability to detect and attack humans.

Professor in het Panhuis said the fins will be made with 3D-printed composite material embedded with electronic devices, including an electromagnetic system.

Electromagnetism targets a shark's highly sensitive electro-sensory system located on its nose by causing sensory overload that results in discomfort and involuntary muscle spasms, often prompting the animal to turn away.

> "By putting an electric field in the fins, that creates a field around surfboards that can confuse the sharks rather than attract them,"

Professor in het Panhuis said.

One of the biggest challenges so far had been working out how to combine each smart device within a fin that could be attached to various surfboards.

![Man wearing lab protective eyewear, smiling in front of surfboards](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/64adc85a280f847c33375a25403a4bc1?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=42&width=862&height=575)

Originally from the Netherlands, Marc in het Panhuis says his career in surf science emerged from his "hopeless love" of the sport. (ABC Illawarra: Justin Huntsdale)

"\[Surfboard fins\] usually weigh up to 100 grams, so there's not a lot of space involved," Professor in het Panhuis said.

> "We're also trying to put performance measurements in there that allows … you to track how fast you are going and how critical your turn."

## Potential game changer, says survivor

Shark attack survivor turned motivational speaker Brett Connellan said if the technology worked, it could allow surfers and sharks to safely co-exist in the water.

"I don't have any hate towards sharks, and I'd love to see a world in which people can learn to co-exist," he said.

"Technology like this, I think, is a great step in the right direction to give people the confidence that if they do go to the beach, they can either make a choice themselves as to the risks they are going to take."

![Large scar on left leg, darkened background](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f62794b4cbb4412aa924d4f1b3358e00?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Brett Connellan lost three-quarters of his left quad, was minutes from death, and was told he might never walk again. (Supplied: Sam Tolhurst)

Surgeons managed to reconstruct Mr Connellan's thigh by transplanting part of his back muscle to replace the lost quadricep after a great white shark bit his leg at Bombo Beach 10 years ago.

The Berry local has since learnt to walk and surf again.

"Having that peace of mind that you are doing that extra 'turning the odds in your favour' if you will is something I could get behind, and I hope a lot of other surfers could as well," he said.

> "As long as the fins are made of, in most cases fibreglass … I can't see too many issues as far as performance arising."

![Blonde man in wet suit standing on beach](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/07da6b43a8baae33333f081b24f577cf?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=533&cropW=800&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Brett Connellan has previously helped Marc in het Panhuis with his work on 3D-printed surfboard fins. (Supplied: Sam Tolhurst)

## Ocean, shark testing

The hope is to eventually manufacture and sell the smart fins as a shark-safe product following testing.

Professor in het Panhuis said it would start with trialling the fins and surfboards in the ocean.

Later stages could involve trials in the presence of marine animals, but only where justified and under strict ethical oversight.

Any testing involving surfers would require approval through the UOW’s human research ethics processes, while trials involving marine life would also require clearance from the relevant animal ethics committee.

Director of Flinders University Marine and Coastal Research Consortium Charlie Huveneers said any new shark-attack mitigation measure was welcome and added to a growing pool of personal shark deterrents.

![A man in a dive suit and snorkelling mask, half submerged in the water](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6b2d7ddda6c431f3e1f37901688cb3ea?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2796&cropW=4194&xPos=1&yPos=20&width=862&height=575)

Charlie Huveneers is testing the efficacy of shark bite mitigation measures. (ABC News: Lincoln Rothall)

But Professor Huveneers said he would prefer the new fins be tested "with potentially dangerous sharks to determine how much it can reduce shark bites".

"A few personal deterrents have been scientifically and independently tested on large predatory sharks," he said.

> "While electric deterrents were the most effective by far, not all electric deterrents are equal, and some electric fields do not affect shark behaviour sufficiently to reduce the risk of shark bites."

## Broader marine safety application

Professor in het Panhuis said the project was not just about creating smart surfboard fins.

"The bigger vision is that this technology can be applied much more broadly — not just to surfboards, but to other watercraft, diving equipment, and surf rescue systems," he said.

"Current NSW SharkSmart measures like drones, tagging and drum lines are about detecting and managing sharks at a beach or regional level.

> "What we're developing sits alongside that as an additional layer of safety at the individual level."

![Wires embedded into blue surfboard](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f43e8dc55d03e2d18abf24a19a917857?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

UOW Surf Flex Lab has previously embedded shark-deterrent technology inside surfboards, but is now trying to replicate that in fins. (ABC Illawarra: Justin Huntsdale)

A NSW Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development spokesperson said in addition to its 2025–26 shark management program, it has funded research exploring the effectiveness of personal deterrents and published those findings in a [Fact Sheet](https://www.sharksmart.nsw.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/1237015/sms-factsheet-personal-protection.pdf).

"The department recommends that surfers and swimmers consider using independently, scientifically tested personal deterrents," a spokesperson said.

"Other personal deterrents will be added to the fact sheet if they are determined to be effective by appropriate scientific studies."

# Rebel Wilson requested website describing producer as an 'Indian Ghislaine Maxwell', court hears

- [![Isobel Roe with white earrings and maroon suit](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/821174528244abf8817bc2019d49e865?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3749&cropW=3749&xPos=626&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
I\\
\\
By Isobel Roe](https://www.abc.net.au/news/isobel-roe/6497722)
- [Topic:Courts](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/courts)

11h ago11 hours agoTue 21 Apr 2026 at 2:12am

![Rebel Wilson arrives at court](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ad8f0c844329d37f6c6df8b7dbe4a43f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=260&width=862&height=485)

Rebel Wilson is being sued for defamation by actor Charlotte MacInnes. (AAP: Dean Lewins)

## In short:

Rebel Wilson is being sued for defamation by actor Charlotte MacInnes, who stars in Ms Wilson's new movie The Deb.

Ms MacInnes has alleged Ms Wilson defamed her in posts on social media by claiming the young actor lied about an incident involving producer Amanda Ghost.

Ms Wilson has been accused of organising a website containing false and malicious claims about Ms MacInnes and Ms Ghost.

Australian actor Rebel Wilson asked her crisis communications firm to publish a website that accused a producer of her own film of being the "Indian Ghislaine Maxwell", a Sydney court has heard.

The Federal Court is hearing a defamation case brought by Charlotte MacInnes, the lead actor in Ms Wilson's latest movie The Deb, in which she alleges Ms Wilson claimed she walked back allegations of being sexually harassed by producer Amanda Ghost, in exchange for acting jobs.

In her statement of claim, Ms MacInnes said her professional relationship with Ms Wilson started to break down early on in the making of The Deb.

Ms MacInnes claims Ms Wilson fabricated a story about Ms Ghost mistreating her in September 2023, and posted it publicly on her Instagram.

![Charlotte MacInnes leaves the Federal Court of Australia ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/b1a25a84da1fbe0f6b0a77edb048caff?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Charlotte MacInnes (right) claims Ms Wilson defamed her in an Instagram post. (AAP: Dean Lewins)

She also accuses Ms Wilson of organising a website with false and malicious claims about her and Ms Ghost.

Ms Wilson, who was not in court on Tuesday, will deny the imputations of her posts were defamatory.

## Court hears details of alleged smear campaign

On Tuesday, the court heard from Katie Case, a former vice-president at US based firm The Agency Group (TAG), which was handling crisis communications for Ms Wilson.

Text messages tendered to the court show Ms Case received a text from TAG chief executive Melissa Nathan in August 2024, telling her that Ms Wilson wanted "one of those sites".

> "It can be really really harsh… making her a madam basically," Ms Nathan texted Ms Case.
>
> "Oh my god lol ok this one will be fun," Ms Case replied in a text.

Court documents allege the site was published at the addresses amandaghost.com and amandaghostsucks.com, and accused Ms Ghost of being a 'madam'.

![ Rebel Wilson arrives at the Federal Court of Australia](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a4063383acd567c5e6800dddd5a8cb5f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1150&cropW=1725&xPos=222&yPos=290&width=862&height=575)

Ms Wilson was not in court for the hearing on Tuesday. (AAP: Adelaide Lang )

"We're in this until Amanda Ghost is exposed for the evil that she is," the website's copy read.

"As the Indian Ghislaine Maxwell, she trades on pleasure for protection…

"Failing in music, she turned full pimp, reinventing herself as a theatrical producer alongside her husband while really procuring young women for the pleasure of the extremely wealthy."

Ghislaine Maxwell was in 2022 sentenced to 20 years in jail for helping her associate Jeffrey Epstein sexually abuse teenage girls.

Court documents also said the website claimed Ms Ghost started The Great Gatsby stage musical "to simply reward an actress for remaining silent about the abuse she suffered at Ghost's hands".

![Three women stand in front of a stage with blue and red velvet curtains](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/90a541a32d8c0f6bb58a16b68ad9951f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Ms MacInnes claims her professional relationship with Ms Wilson started to break down early on in the making of The Deb. (Supplied: John Platt)

Ms Case told the court her PR firm called it a "whistleblower site", which was published with the purpose of encouraging anyone with information about the subject of the site to come forward.

Justice Elizabeth Raper asked Ms Case what she meant by whistleblower site.

> "What you mean is, there's no actual whistleblower," Justice Raper asked Ms Case.
>
> "It's just created to look like it is?"
>
> "That's correct," Ms Case replied.

Ms Case told the court she was asked to edit the copy for the site.

Under cross-examination from Ms Wilson's counsel, Ms Case told the court she could not be entirely sure who wrote the initial copy; just that Melissa Nathan sent it to her for editing.

In her defence filed in court, Ms Wilson denied that there were defamatory meanings conveyed by the posts and website.

If the court finds they were defamatory, Ms Wilson's legal case will rely partially on the defence of truth.

## The incident at Bondi Beach

The sexual harassment claim Ms MacInnes denies was ever true, centred around an incident at Bondi Beach on September 5, 2023.

Lawyers for Ms MacInnes said she and Ms Ghost were swimming at Bondi Beach, when Ms Ghost had a severe medical reaction to the cold water and had a bath back at her apartment to warm up.

Ms Wilson claims Ms MacInnes raised concerns at work the next day about feeling uncomfortable when Ms Ghost asked the young actress to get in the bath with her.

But Ms MacInnes claims she was never concerned, and a complaint was never made.

The trial is expected to go over nine days.
